summary: Stancil uses his anxiety about flying to argue that emotions often overpower facts, making rational self-talk an unreliable defense against fear. He warns that modern AI, especially chatbots, is uniquely adept at shaping feelings, so the larger risk is not misinformation but engineered emotional manipulation at scale.
money_quotes: I can’t bend my feelings around facts; I can’t forge them between a hammer of reason and the anvil of reality.
Those were stilted and sterile, like an uncomfortably formal support agent who’s required to read from an unnatural script.
But even in these early days, ChatGPT and its siblings are fluid and anthropomorphic; they can, as Thompson says, “make the human it is interacting with feel something.”
In other words, the entire point of Tome, and dozens of other apps like it, is emotional capture.
If this is where we arrive, it’s hard to see who’s controlling who—are we in charge of the AI, or is the AI in charge of us?
key_insight: AI’s greatest near-term power is its ability to manipulate human emotion, and our weakness against that manipulation makes collective harm more likely than mere confusion about facts.
